summary: O'Neil reports from the 2019 Joint Mathematics Meeting where she received the MAA Euler Book Prize and reconnected with the math community. She reflects on the state of academic mathematics, noting she's less worried about MOOCs disrupting teaching than she once was, but remains concerned about the adjunctification of faculty positions. She also critiques the slow pace of change in mathematical publishing and credentialing systems, wondering when collaborative projects like the Stacks Project will become the norm.
key_points: The MOOC threat to traditional math teaching has not materialized as feared
The replacement of tenured faculty with adjunct staff continues to erode quality of life for math PhDs
Mathematical publishing and credentialing systems remain stuck in metric-gaming patterns
Collaborative open projects like the Stacks Project represent an obvious but unrealized future for mathematical contributions
The math community remains warm and welcoming despite institutional challenges
money_quotes: I registered for this year's Joint Math Meeting by claiming to be Press so I think it's only fair that I blog from the conference.
Math nerds are and always will be unbelievably adorable.

I was quite worried, for many years, that MOOCs and other 'flipped classroom' type scenarios would take over calculus teaching. I'm no longer so worried about that, because I simply haven't heard of it working on a broad scale.
The profession of academic mathematics is not a growing or improving field in terms of quality of life for the median Ph.D. grad.
It seems like there's just as much gaming, counting, and other kind of dumb metric stuff going on as ever.

I'm wondering when people will start seriously contributing to things like the Stack Project – and figure out a way of giving credit to people for those contributions – because it seems like the obvious future of mathematical contributions.
key_insight: While the MOOC threat to math education has faded, academic mathematics still faces structural problems in how it treats faculty and credentials contributions.
